data.title: The Cycle Persists Through Geopolitical Disruption
data.description:
By John Hillenbrand, CPA

The US economy entered the year in a mid-cycle expansion supported by fiscal tailwinds, easing monetary policy, and a capital-spending boom driven by artificial intelligence. The war in Iran and its effects on energy markets have introduced competing forces: higher commodity prices squeezing real incomes, tighter financial conditions, and renewed uncertainty over supply chains. The central question for investors is whether this shock is large enough to end the expansion or is a disruption that the cycle can absorb. We believe it is the latter. Growth may slow but not contract, the labor market is softening but not breaking, headline inflation is rising but core remains contained, and capital spending continues.

Our January outlook argued that above-consensus US growth would persist as the tariff drag faded, fiscal support from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) flowed through, and the Fed eased toward short-term rates of 3.00% to 3.25%. Economic data through February confirmed that view. The war in Iran disrupted the trajectory in March, with a rise of more than 50% in the price of oil, a 60-basis point tightening in financial conditions, and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The fund (CGIIX) returned –3.8% for the quarter, ahead of the S&P 500 Index’s –4.3% decline. Selection within equities contributed positively, although the portfolio’s elevated equity sensitivity, consistent with our mid-cycle posture, was a headwind in a down market.

Growth Drivers Remain Intact

The positive forces supporting economic expansion have not been impaired. Lagged effects of last year’s rate cuts are still flowing through, fiscal support from the OBBBA is additive to consumer and corporate spending, defense and energy security infrastructure investment is accelerating, and the AI infrastructure buildout has become a meaningful driver of the real economy, contributing roughly a third of real-GDP growth last year. The hyperscalers have shown no inclination to pull back; their capital spending guidance has ratcheted higher through each successive reporting season, and their suppliers are converting that spending into earnings at exceptional incremental margins.

The AI investment thesis is evolving in ways that matter for positioning. Coding assistance has achieved strong adoption but has created volatility in application software names as investors debate disruption risk versus embedded AI benefits; we are waiting for earnings to provide more data points before adding there.

More important for the cycle, agentic AI adoption outside of coding is emerging as a growth driver; industries with the highest exposure to LLM-automatable tasks are showing the highest rates of business AI deployment, suggesting companies are finding productive applications for real-world labor tasks. Early evidence suggests companies deploying these models at scale are converting adoption into revenue at rates that support the spending trajectory. We are monitoring for the inflection point where hyperscaler cloud and AI services’ profit growth outpace capital spending growth, which would validate the return-on-investment case.

Risks and the Inflation Question

The forces opposing the expansion are real but, in our assessment, manageable. Higher energy prices are squeezing real incomes, with the burden falling disproportionately on the bottom 60% of the income distribution, where the combined effects of tariffs, benefit adjustments, and the oil shock amount to roughly a full percentage point drag on disposable income. Central banks may delay rate cuts as they assess the persistence of the energy price shock. Private debt default rates running at several multiples of commercial bank loan book defaults are a source of stress if capital markets tighten. The labor market is softening but not breaking, with slowing demand balancing reduced supply from immigration restrictions. That balance is fragile.

The critical distinction in our framework is between a headline inflation shock that remains confined to energy and one that broadens into wages and inflation expectations. We see more parallels with 1990–91 and 2000, where energy shocks occurred against moderate demand and roughly neutral policy, than with 2021–22, when broad supply disruptions coincided with expansionary policy and tight labor markets. The oil intensity of US GDP has declined substantially, domestic shale provides a partial offset, and core disinflation from the waning tariff impact should outweigh the energy price effect over the coming months. This assessment supports our decision to maintain mid-cycle equity sensitivity rather than shift to a defensive posture.

Positioning and Outlook

Our positioning reflects this framework. We concentrated the fund's portfolio in areas where we see real growth tailwinds and improving returns on capital: AI infrastructure and semiconductor equipment, targeted pharma and biotech, energy innovation and security, and international markets where valuations offer favorable entry points. The war in Iran has reinforced the case for domestic energy infrastructure, a theme we had been building before the disruption.

We rotated healthcare to active stock selection where we see improving returns on capital; trimmed rate-sensitive financials as near-term easing expectations shifted; retained core bank holdings where the yield curve thesis remains intact; and took profits on semiconductor convertible winners where the risk-reward had narrowed. We are using convertible securities and options to adjust the portfolio’s risk-reward profile in this environment.

We assign the highest probability to a base case in which the Strait of Hormuz returns to normal operations toward late April, energy prices ease in the second half, and the economic damage remains contained. The upside case sees rapid conflict resolution and accelerating AI monetization; the downside sees the war persisting through mid-year with inflation broadening into core measures.

The catalysts that will determine which path materializes are the trajectory of the war, the inflation data over the next two months, and first-quarter hyperscaler earnings guidance. A favorable resolution would validate a return to the more constructive posture we held entering the year. An extended conflict that pushed inflation into wages and core prices, a decisive break in labor market data, or a downward revision to hyperscaler spending guidance would prompt us to de-risk through the convertible and options sleeves of the portfolio. The mid-cycle thesis remains intact, and we are positioned to participate as clarity returns.

Original Post

data.title: Palantir: Hard To Replace, Harder To Ignore At The Core Of Business AI
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Artificial Intelligence is scaling in layers and the AI stalwarts are all playing an important role.

For example, NVIDIA (NVDA) increases the computing power. Furthermore, companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) convert the power into enterprise platforms. Then there is OpenAI which functions to keep lifting model capability.

Enterprise deployment is the problem in this chain. Most companies are unable to transform AI into something that can be implemented in everyday business. The majority of AI projects fail to pass testing due to messy data, increased costs or risk controls.

Palantir (PLTR) is the solution to this issue, to say the least.

Its Artificial Intelligence Platform links decisions, workflow and results in a seamless manner and becomes core to daily operations. The more it is used by the teams, the more difficult it is to substitute it.

This dependency is already reflected in the numbers. The momentum continued through Q4 FY 25 as Palantir closed some big deals and current clients increased usage along with budgets. With time, growth goes further into workflows and makes the platform a fundamental aspect of how businesses are run.

The momentum is also transferred into the stock price. PLTR trades at around $148 (as of writing), and has support at around $143-$145. This arrangement is in line with the business trend and short-term signals show that it has more upside.

Basically, the platform is so ingrained in everyday business that the risk is mitigated even when growth decelerates slightly. The Maven program-of-record is also a hot catalyst and it would also provide stability to the company.

Overall, I assign PLTR a Strong Buy at this point.

Palantir Sits at the Control Layer

Palantir’s Ontology lies at the heart of its system and bridges decisions in the enterprise to the real world.

After that, it transforms those decisions into workflows with data writeback. That puts it between data, models and execution and forms a layer that is difficult to replace. The process of integration increases with time and dependency begins to form.

Nevertheless, in zooming out, Enterprise AI governance is the bigger prospect.

Most AI projects fail post-testing as discussed earlier and companies are also not able to see clear results in many situations.

Palantir allows companies to work with various models, with some level of control. Thus, when orchestration is the primary point of bottleneck, the company controlling that layer captures the value.

Interestingly, Ryan Taylor, Palantir’s Chief Revenue Officer, claimed in the Q4 FY 25 earnings call,

“Our customers aren't tentatively trying AI; they're committing to it at scale with Palantir Technologies Inc. as the driving force.”

This is supported by the figures. The company has ended up closing 180 deals above $1 million and 61 deals above $10 million in Q4. Well, this suggests a huge enterprise demand.

The total contract value was up to $4.26 billion in terms of the pipeline of committed business.

Net dollar retention came in at 139%, which means existing customers are expanding their usage. Moreover, revenue per top 20 customer jumped 45% YOY to $94 million. These statistics reflect actual adoption on the team and budget levels.

Expansion is not only about the number of new customers. Palantir closed Q4 with 954 customers, a 34% upswing. In the meantime, U.S. commercial revenue bumped 137% YOY.

There is a larger narrative to this. The platform is spreading inside existing accounts. There is an upswing in the number of teams joining the system, an increase in the volume of work traveling through it as well as budget settings.

Honestly speaking, the figures are encouraging and such performance will bring about a sustained competitive edge that will continue to spur growth.

AIP Creates Sticky Organizational Dependency

Palantir creates a moat by getting deeply involved with the companies.

The AIP is confused with a simple LLM interface by most. Nevertheless, the platform is a workflow engine that bridges AI and operational data.

This point of difference is crucial since Palantir is more focused on security and governance measures such as granular permissions and audit trails. All these properties make AIP more of a policy layer, instead of a simple chat box.

With the enormous responsibility claims that come with production AI, this is exactly the kind of guardrails that it is seeking.

The budget is directed towards tools that are trustworthy to the auditors. The NIST AI Risk Management Framework connects the aspect of trustworthiness to risk reduction and Palantir scores in all aspects.

Although the 2025 survey report of McKinsey reveals that close to two out of three organizations still have a hard time scaling AI, Palantir has been successful within such controlled settings. The company has recorded a 57% adjusted operating margin and 56% adjusted free cash flow margin in Q4. Such margins imply that deep integration is profitable.

This scale only increases during 2026 (forward guidance). The adjusted income of operations is projected to be between $4.126 billion and $4.142 billion by the management. The company also steered adjusted FCF to between $3.925 and $4.125 billion.

This really sums up why this platform creates lasting dependency. A business model has reached such an efficiency point when it is generating almost as much cash as it is operating income. This financial capability is based on the dependency that Palantir engineering builds.

The platform represents related decisions using its Ontology, as opposed to raw data points. Once the approval chains and audit trails are integrated into the software by a company, the switching cost will be a nightmare both technically and operationally.

AIP motivates growth through dependence on the platform. It shifts Palantir from a software provider into the real record of operational decisions. When a business has grown to be dependent on this automation in its day-to-day operation, the software is difficult to rip out.

This structural lock-in is a point that should be taken into consideration since it allows Palantir to earn high incremental margins. This combination is the main factor behind the growth compounding that is being projected in the 2026 perspective.

Claude vs. AIP: Why the Control Layer remains Bulletproof

The launch of Claude CoWork at the beginning of 2026 is a gigantic leap into agentic AI. Anthropic essentially transformed a chat box into a proactive digital force. It is a tool that is able to plan, browse, and execute sophisticated projects without human intervention.

Certain investors fear that such autonomous models would one day make Palantir redundant. I believe that is totally misconstrued. Claude is an excellent personal productivity tool, but it does not have the institutional oversight needed in mission-critical work.

Claude is incapable of operating a global supply chain without a strict audit trail. The record of truth and the granular permissions found in the AIP are beyond what standalone agents could possibly handle.

Practically, Palantir would consider models such as Claude to be high-performance engines but offers the chassis and steering wheel to keep them safe on the road. The layer that coordinates the models is the actual monopoly as models are commoditized.

This lock-in makes Palantir resilient even if the models become more autonomous. It is what distinguishes a smart assistant and a controlled operating system.

Palantir Secures Its Place in the Military Arsenal

Similar to how Palantir enters enterprise workflows, it is now doing so to military operations with the Maven program-of-record.

Palantir has just gotten the final Pentagon seal of approval. Last month, the Deputy Secretary of Defense officially designated the Maven Smart System as a program of record.

This is enormous since it makes Maven more than an experiment and solidly entrenched in the military arsenal with funding. The Pentagon believes that this shift will equip warfighters with the required instruments to identify and control the enemy as well as guarantee a steady stream of resources into the platform.

Consider Maven to be the main command and control brain of modern warfare. It combines huge volumes of data from satellites, drones, and radars to provide actionable intelligence on the fly.

Enemy vehicles and missile stockpiles are detected by the system at an incredibly high rate, and this greatly reduces the decision-making process on the battlefield.

Originally, in 2024 Palantir was awarded a contract worth $480 million, however, the demand soared and the Defense Department had to increase its limit to 1.3 billion by 2025. The fact that the military is counting on it for thousands of real blows is obvious.

This record status program serves as an enormous catalyst for the company. It ensures a steady flow of revenue, and Maven becomes part of the long-term military strategy. This justifies the ontology-based platform as the desirable operating system for defense workflows.

Most contractors come and go around the next budget cycle, but Palantir has just made it into the must fund list. This approval secures years of high-value contracts and provides the firm with enormous power over the future of global defense technology.

Valuation Discussion

Palantir sells at premium multiples with its forward P/E estimated at 112x for FY 26 and 80x for FY 27. These figures are projected under consensus EPS of around $1.34 and $1.89 in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

It seems like the overstretched multiples explain everything. However, to me, the market is confusing category with true value.

These estimates already include high operational momentum and increasing enterprise adoption, with revisions shifting upwards as demand picks up.

A superior lens is FCF. Palantir has generated adjusted FCF margins over 50%, which is a rare mix where profitability and growth scale together. This type of effectiveness, along with profound enterprise integration, enables the company to transform incremental revenue into disproportionate cash production.

In future, Palantir is expected to maintain 30% to 35% revenue growth per year. Such anticipation is on the back of higher adoption among current customers and consistent acquisition of new ones. It is projected that revenue will increase from about $7.2 billion in FY 26 to approximately $10.5 billion in FY 27.

The company may generate between $6 to $7 billion annually with long-term FCF margins of 40% to 45%. Using a 25x to 30x cash flow multiple would justify a valuation that compounds significantly out of the present levels.

The premium is justified and there are actual signs of the platform scaling within operations as opposed to being optional.

The stock trades around $148 (as of writing), which has support at $143 to $145. The resistance is close to $162 and $164. Notably, weekly RSI has fallen below the overbought status, and there is room for upside without immediate pressure.

The base case is pretty simple. It has Palantir pushing to $155 to 160, with consistent client growth. Bull case may go up to $170 to $180 in case there is faster adoption of enterprise AI and big deals are closed more quickly.

Even in less favorable circumstances, integration into day-to-day operations maintains a floor near $140-$145.

The market still fails to read Palantir correctly due to the tendency of the analysts to evaluate software based on the number of features and seats sold.

Palantir is able to create sustainable cash flows and retention by becoming a part of core operations, which can underpin a re-rating even if headline growth dips.

Risks To My Thesis and Takeaway

Despite the high control-layer advantage of Palantir, there are still a few risks.

First and foremost, the adoption of the enterprise can still be restricted. Unless Palantir can incorporate its platform into everyday tasks completely, revenue retention and expansion in large accounts would be slack, which would be tracked by adoption metrics.

Secondly, AI governance may turn into a commodity. The cloud providers are also developing compliance capabilities on their platforms and cross platform tools are gaining popularity. When governance becomes a standard expectation, then Palantir can experience pricing pressure despite growth in usage. It is prudent to note how the customers use the platform i.e., as a decision engine or a convenience tool.

Lastly, the dependency on government contracts makes it sensitive to changes in policy and procurement. In FY 25, the majority of revenue was in the form of public sector work, and establishing solutions at the organization level takes time and resources. Delayed deployments (an execution risk) would be a burden to margins.

Indeed, challenges are there but the history of Palantir argues that it can cope with them.

The emphasis on adoption and persistent growth helps to understand that Palantir is an essential part of how businesses function.

Being invested is real progress as the platform finds its way in the organization. This stock rewards those who appreciate the long-term benefit.

data.title: Energy Transfer Is Still A Golden Goose
data.description:
Background

In late 2024, I recommended investors buy Energy Transfer LP (ET) for its reinvigorated balance sheet, growth potential, and well-supported distribution. Since then, ET's 36% total return was nearly double the 19% total return of the S&P 500 Index (SP500). Many investors are rightfully thrilled; the company's distribution yield is still over 7%, and the distribution is expected to increase 3-5% over the next year. Growth investors will be thrilled too; earnings grew more than 10% year over year, and the company plowed about $4.5B into growth CapEx in 2025.

Leverage And Dilution

Even while growth and increased distributions are broadly positive, the balance sheet counts too. Generally, limited partnerships like Energy Transfer can fund minimal growth with cash, but debt or dilution are often required for more aggressive growth. Aggressive growth is characteristic of Energy Transfer, and hopefully, investors will not be horribly shocked that the company chose to add substantial debt. Net debt and share counts are plotted over the last five quarters below.

The good news is that dilution is minimal, with total units increasing by only about 0.5%. At the same time, net debt increased around 15%, from roughly $60B to about $69B. Ballooning debt could be problematic, especially given Energy Transfer's history and concerns around excessive leverage.

However, let's allow that $9B in new debt could be fantastic news if invested and managed well. Or more specifically, if revenues, distribution coverage, and EBITDA are at least keeping pace with debt such that leverage and distribution coverage can reasonably be expected to improve over time.

In other words, what kind of fool would not want to own a growing golden goose with expanding revenue, bigger distributions, lower leverage, and greater potential for price return?

Just The Numbers, Please

I think the narrative approach is well covered by the company's investor materials and other analysts. I'm going to take the numbers approach and focus on the company's annual reports with a little help from Seeking Alpha's consensus forward estimates.

Likewise, I will be referencing historic data sourced from both Energy Transfer's yearly reports and Seeking Alpha's financial sheets. I will also reference YE26 projections based on Seeking Alpha consensus estimates, average margins, and historic rates of change. These data, their sources, and associated notes are tabulated below.

Revenue and Earnings

Energy Transfer's historical and consensus estimated revenue and earnings are plotted from YE23 through YE26.

Over the last two years, revenue grew about 8%, while earnings grew 11% overall despite declining slightly in 2025. Seeking Alpha consensus estimates call for YE26 revenue to grow almost 30%, from $83.8B to $107B. At the same time, YE26 earnings per share are expected to increase over 25% versus YE25 to $1.52/share.

Distributions And Distribution Coverage

Energy Transfer and other limited partnerships generally quantify their distribution coverage with their own uniquely defined distribution coverage ratios. While those company-specific metrics can be useful, I prefer a simple and direct approach with distribution coverage defined in trailing twelve-month cash flow terms as follows:

Distribution Coverage = Cash From Operations/Total Common and Preferred Distributions Paid.

This approach does not account for maintenance CAPEX vs. growth investments or any other adjustments. However, if this simple ratio remains above 2.0 and leverage remains reasonable, competent management and robust distribution coverage are generally indicated.

Over the last two years, cash from operations has increased about 6%, while total distributions have increased 11%; distribution coverage has averaged about 2.3X. Based on estimated revenue and historic margins, YE26 cash from operations is expected to increase about 30%. Allowing for distributions to grow 5% in 2026, distribution coverage will still expand to 2.8 times.

Net Debt and Leverage

We have already discussed Energy Transfer's aggressive growth posture and growing debt; net debt grew by $8.6B to $68.8B in 2025. Assuming net debt grows another $8.6B in 2026, it will reach $77.4B.

Net debt has averaged about 4.3 times EBITDA over the last three years. Given the historic EBITDA margin and estimated YE26 revenue, EBITDA is expected to grow over 20%, with leverage contracting to just 4.2 over the next year.

Energy Transfer Appears Undervalued

Energy Transfer's price-to-sales and PE ratios are plotted alongside those of its closest midstream peers by market cap: Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), Enbridge Inc. (ENB), Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI), TC Energy Corporation (TRP), and The Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB).

Energy Transfer is currently valued at just 0.78 times revenue and 15.6 times earnings. These ratios currently exceed their own five-year averages but fall substantially below peer average price-to-sales and P/E ratios. Based on estimated YE26 revenue and earnings, Energy Transfer's price-to-sales and PE ratios are expected to contract to just above their own five-year averages over the next year.

If we assume Energy Transfer's price/sales and P/E ratios will expand towards the average ratios of its peers, share prices have plenty of room to run. Based on YE26 consensus earnings and peer average PE ratio, a fair value share price estimate can be calculated as follows:

Price/share = YE26 estimated earnings * peer average P/E = $1.52/share * 24.0 = $36.48/share.

Nearly 100% upside is implied versus the current price at $18.93/share.

Conclusions and Recommendations

While valuation ratios like PE are simple to calculate, they are also a broad reflection of investor sentiment. Sentiment is harder to quantify, but Energy Transfer's relatively low valuation is not without cause. Historically, market concerns around Energy Transfer included its aggressive growth, excess leverage, and thinly covered distributions. In fact, the company cut its dividend in late 2020.

In late 2024, I dug into Energy Transfer's leverage, cash flow, and distribution coverage. I concluded the company was back on solid ground and poised for sustainable growth; I recommended investors buy the company's stock. More recently, the company has returned to growth with expanding debt. This time around, revenue, earnings, EBITDA, and cash flow are also expanding. Consequently, leverage and distribution coverage remain favorable and are expected to improve further over 2026.

I expect market sentiment to improve around Energy Transfer as it continues to grow its revenues and distributions while maintaining a strong balance sheet. Improved sentiment is likely to drive the company's valuation ratios to expand towards the average ratios of the company's peers, implying substantial share price return. In short, Energy Transfer is still a golden goose; I recommend investors buy at current market prices.

data.title: Energy Shock Set To Push Building Material Prices Higher
data.description:
By Maurice van Sante, Senior Economist Construction & Team Lead Sectors

More price increases expected

Many sectors will be impacted by the current surge in energy prices caused by the conflict in the Middle East. As noted previously, producers of building materials such as cement, concrete and bricks rely heavily on energy. If energy prices remain high, these manufacturers are likely to pass increased costs on to construction companies, which will put pressure on profit margins and lead to higher overall building costs. This, in turn, could result in reduced production volumes.

Early indications point to rising building material costs. In March, a net 18% of EU building materials suppliers anticipated increasing their sales prices over the next three months. This figure represents the highest percentage observed in three years since the energy crisis of 2022. Yet, the number doesn’t come close to the levels of the energy crisis in 2022.

Different energy sources among countries

Heating sources differ among producers across European countries. For example, gas is commonly used in the building material sector in the Netherlands and Spain. In contrast, producers in Poland and Germany depend more on coal, wood, natural energy inputs and energy residuals. This makes building material producers in the Netherlands and Spain relatively vulnerable to the current price increases in gas. Dutch producers are also reporting the strongest expected increase in output prices.

Building material production at a low level

The rising cost of energy is hitting the building materials sector at a challenging time. Many countries have experienced significant production declines over the past few years, largely because fewer new residential and non-residential buildings are being constructed. Building permits for new homes have shown an uptick since the end of 2025, offering some positive signs for the sector. Yet ongoing market uncertainty, increased material costs and hesitant consumers may threaten this positive trend.

Decline of oil dependency, but not in the last five years

In the period 2010-2020, the amount of oil as a heating source has decreased substantially in the sector, although it hasn’t witnessed a further decrease in the last five years. Between 2020-2025, companies mainly phased out the use of coal, while the relative amount of used gas has remained more or less the same for the last 15 years. Building material companies have become more sustainable due to the reduced reliance on coal, but the dependency on oil and gas hasn’t really decreased. The higher energy costs are therefore still a burden to the industry.

UK and Sweden frontrunners

Within Europe, the UK and Swedish building material producers stand out for having made strides in lowering their energy intensity. The UK, in particular, has taken major steps by phasing out oil and coal from production. For instance, UK Hanson Cement (part of HeidelbergCement (HDLMY)) has sharply reduced coal use at its Ribblesdale plant and replaced it with biomass and waste-derived fuel. In Sweden, reductions in energy intensity have been more evenly spread across coal, gas, and oil inputs.

Fossil fuel risks persist in building materials

The European building materials sector still faces significant challenges from higher energy costs and new market uncertainty. While the reliance on oil and coal has decreased in the last two decades, the dependency on gas has remained more or less the same. In addition, the dependency on oil hasn’t decreased since 2020.

The recent uptick in building permits offers a glimmer of hope for stronger demand, but a sustained recovery will depend on greater stability in energy markets and continued innovation in sustainable production methods. Without this, rising production costs risk pushing sales prices higher, which, in turn, would weigh on demand. A firm commitment to greener, less energy-intensive production processes will therefore be essential for long-term resilience.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: FuboTV's Surge Means Mr. Market Is Finally Growing On This Show
data.description:
April 6th was a really fantastic day for shareholders of streaming company FuboTV (FUBO). Shares of the stocks skyrocketed 23.5% after management provided an upbeat outlook when it comes to operating performance over the next few years. It wasn't all that long ago that I was incredibly bearish about the company. Back in February of 2024, for instance, I said that the firm was facing an existential crisis. I remained bearish about it until an unexpected turn took place. This involved, in January of 2025, the news that The Walt Disney Company (DIS) had decided to contribute to FuboTV some cash, as well as its Hulu + Live TV business in what was certainly a sizable deal that changed everything for the business. In my most recent article, I expressed bullishness about the company, with future prospects likely to be rosy following the close of that combination between FuboTV and Disney's assets. And yet, even with this recent jump in share price, shares are down since I last wrote about it in November of last year.

The good news is that the data provided by management on April 6th points to what I would consider to be a very bright future for the business. With Disney owning a 70% stake in the firm, I don't see a scenario where it goes under. It also helps that the business has cash that exceeds the amount of debt that it has by $72.7 million. And when you add on top of this how the stock is priced on a forward basis, it's difficult to argue against the 'buy' rating I assigned it late last year.

Taking a gander at FuboTV

It's rare that shares of a company urge 23.5% or even some number similar to that on any given day. Often, these moves up are the result of a buyout, a merger, or some other value-accretive transaction. But that was not the case when it came to FuboTV. Instead, management released a shareholder letter detailing what the next few years for the business should look like. The most important part of the latter, in my view, was forward guidance regarding EBITDA. On a pro forma basis that incorporates Hulu + Live TV, the streaming platform generated EBITDA in the trailing 12 months ending in September of last year in the amount of $58.5 million. But for this year, management anticipates that figure to grow to between $80 million and $100 million. But that's nothing. In 2028, the firm expects EBITDA of at least $300 million.

Management provided some details regarding their expectations here. For starters, the firm stands to benefit from contractually obligated wholesale fees. In essence, during the term of its commercial agreement with Hulu, FuboTV is entitled to receive a wholesale fee at a ratio to Hulu + Live TV's carriage costs. That ratio is 95% for this year, 97.5% next year, and 99% in 2028 and beyond. For context, when it comes to the streaming and pay TV market, a carriage fee is the fee that a distributor pays to channels, networks, and other parties, to host their content. Most of this money is essentially being transferred from Hulu to FuboTV, meaning that additional cash flow should come to the business as a result of these activities.

But that's not all. Management continues to expect lower content costs as time goes on, not to mention a slew of additional content that should draw additional users in. There is definitely some credibility to this claim. In late March of this year, for instance, the company announced an agreement with Spectrum SportsNet LA whereby the Los Angeles Dodgers will see their content stream live on the company's platform at the start of the 2026 Major League Baseball season. This will involve the airing of more than 140 season games.

Just a day after that deal was announced, FuboTV made public the fact that it struck a multi-year distribution agreement with the Atlanta Braves that will see the company provide BravesVision to its customers. This also involves over 140 games each season. Stuff like this brings in additional sports fans, which naturally translates to additional revenue. Management also informed investors that the company has started to market its Hulu + Live TV service to customers who may prefer its more comprehensive channel lineup. This arrangement will see customers get Hulu + Live TV for three months at $64.99 per month as opposed to the standard $89.99 per month.

When it comes to valuing the company, there is some guesswork that needs to be taken into consideration. For instance, management has not provided pro forma figures or estimates when it comes to adjusted operating cash flow, which, I believe, is superior to EBITDA. But by factoring in the current interest cost that the company incurs, as well as making other adjustments, rough calculations for what adjusted operating cash flow should be for 2026 and 2028, as well as what it likely was for the most recent trailing 12 month window.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced and how it is priced on a forward basis. As you can tell, shares get considerably cheaper if management can achieve the targets that they have laid out. But there is another way to value the company also. To be honest with you, there really aren't very many streaming platforms that you could say are truly pureplay. I would say that the closest one would be Netflix (NFLX). But of course, with a market capitalization at $416.6 billion, and significant positive cash flow, the company is certainly leagues apart from FuboTV from an operational standpoint. Having said that, in the chart below, you can see that there exists a massive disparity in valuation between the two firms on a market capitalization to subscriber basis and on an enterprise value to subscriber basis. When you consider the improvements that management is forecasting for the next few years, it's certainly not unreasonable to expect for some of this spread to narrow. Although this occurred back in 2021, another comparable that we could look at is when AT&T (T) sold off its anime streaming service Crunchyroll to Sony (SONY). That was at a price of $1.175 billion. With three million paid subscribers at the time, that translated to $391.67 on an EV to EBITDA basis. So even in respect to that, FuboTV is trading quite cheap.

Takeaway

The data that is available at our disposal right now is, in my view, fascinating. There is no guarantee about what the future holds. But clearly, management has an optimistic view of matters. If we take them for their word, we can see that shares of the business do get cheap rather quickly. And already, in some respects, they are cheap by streaming platform standards. Considering these factors, I do believe that maintaining the company as a 'buy' makes sense here.

data.title: Table-Pounding Fat Dividend Picks In This Fearful Market
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

The volatility of a public market where share prices are moving every day in reaction to often irrelevant news items should be an unmitigated positive for investors. Yet so often, it isn't.

Investing has become "gamified," with many investors approaching the market like a game where they have to watch and push the buy or sell button at the right time. They don't view themselves as investing in companies or the success of a sector. Instead, they are placing a wager that the price will go up within a certain amount of time. If it doesn't go up, then they "lost."

In my opinion, that is a very unhealthy way to invest. It often leads to frequent trading, where the fundamentals of the companies don't really matter. It's just a ticker, and the price is changing. It doesn't matter whether the ticker represents a profitable business or a meme coin that was invented just for laughs. Investors are just looking to buy and click the sell button before the price goes down.

My investment strategy is to invest in businesses that do something that produces a profit. They aren't random tickers trading at random prices. They are businesses where people go to work every day, do something to produce value, and generate a profit. A portion of that profit is shared with me through dividends.

The trading price fluctuates, but the reality of the underlying business is based on macroeconomic factors that impact their profitability, not the whims of the market.

Let's take a look at two ways you can invest in companies that are producing tangible results.

Pick #1: ARCC – Yield 10.6%

Ares Capital (ARCC) is a business development company, or BDC. It is one of the oldest holdings in our HDO Model Portfolio and has been a mainstay that we have talked about a lot over the years.

It's also down 25% from 52-week highs.

If you read the comments on articles talking about BDCs and the alleged dangers of "private credit". Oh no! The sky is falling! Prices are down! BDCs are dangerous!

People who loved BDCs when they were expensive are now downgrading them while they are cheap. We are guilty of not realizing gains. We don't really see a reason to sell an investment that is producing stable and reliable income. When ARCC was expensive, we wrote:

ARCC is a BDC that definitely deserves to trade at a premium to NAV. Given its size and its slow and steady growth, that premium should be in the 110–120% range, in our opinion. That range tends to align with where ARCC's price typically trends toward in the long run. At current NAV, that is a range of $21.89 to $23.88. If it is trading above that range, we'd recommend being patient. If it is below that range, then it's a bargain worth going out of the way for. As I write this, ARCC is trading at a fair valuation and is a powerful long-term workhorse that should be part of your portfolio.

Fast forward to today, and ARCC is now trading well below that range. It's trading at a price it touched in 2022. What did we do in 2022? We bought. It was trading well below it in 2020, and guess what we were doing? We were buying.

Some people really want to make investing far more complicated than it needs to be. They have systems where you have to check the moon phase, see if your dog's fur is tilting left or right, and check on what Punxsutawney Phil did to determine whether you should buy.

We make the question much more simple:

  1. Is ARCC a good investment that we want to own?
  2. Is it trading at an attractive price to buy today?

That's it. Two questions.

We know the answer to question 1; we bought ARCC 10 years ago and have enjoyed periodically adding to it ever since. The reason is simple. ARCC is in the business of lending, and in lending, underwriting and management are everything. Everyone loves to hype up the "risks" of private credit, but the reality is that these risks are very idiosyncratic. Unlike syndicated loans, which are often broken up into small pieces among many different investors, BDCs tend to originate and hold their own loans. The loans that ARCC holds were originated by ARCC. So, if there's a problem with the underwriting over at KKR, how does that impact ARCC? It doesn't.

ARCC's historical results with credit risk have been impeccable. Source

It isn't just a little bit lower than banks; ARCC's credit losses are dramatically lower than banks. From October 2004 through December 2025, a period that includes the GFC, the shale oil bust, and COVID, ARCC's results have been $1 billion in net realized gains.

Meanwhile, banks and BDC peers have averaged realized losses.

Occasionally, some BDC somewhere has bad investments. They experience a wave of defaults, and inevitably, every other BDC sells off too. When this happens, we buy more ARCC. Past results are not a guarantee of future results. However, ARCC has routinely been among the best lenders in the business. Not just the best among BDCs. They run circles around banks as well.

Yes, we want to own ARCC, and if the market is willing to let us "Buy" at a discount to NAV and people are selling their shares, we are happy to buy them and relieve them of the burden of trying to figure out what to spend that big dividend on every quarter.

Pick #2: AWP – Yield 12.7%

abrdn Global Premier Properties Fund (AWP) is a CEF (closed-end fund) that invests in global real estate opportunities. Coming into 2026, AWP had a bias for U.S. REITs, which accounted for slightly over 2/3rds of its portfolio: Source

Its top 10 holdings are led by overweight positions in Welltower (WELL) and Prologis (PLD).

In its Q4 2025 Quarterly Commentary, management wrote:

As a result, we are focused on sectors and companies where we see opportunities to increase rents in the near term, with strong structural tailwinds supporting longer-term growth. We have also increased the Fund’s allocation to less economically sensitive sectors, which we believe will continue to post attractive relative returns.

REITs have struggled worldwide with higher interest rates. It's a sector that frequently uses leverage to amplify returns. Real estate is a business where gains come from slow and steady rents being paid for years. Real estate prices are often correlated with long-term interest rates—coming down when rates are higher and going up when they are lower.

Since Q4, we've seen increased fears of inflation and international conflict. In the short term, these fears have negatively impacted trading prices for real estate. In the mid- to long-term, we expect that high oil prices are more likely to lead to net deflation as higher energy costs slow down global economies. Central banks are likely to respond to an economic slowdown by cutting interest rates. Meanwhile, investors will become more attracted to investments that have an investment profile of long-term and stable cash flows, like rent.

Investor enthusiasm for real estate was hot early in the year, but prices have dipped with the conflict in Iran:

We believe that this is an opportunity to "Buy."

Conclusion

An investment in ARCC benefits from ARCC investing in numerous businesses. It makes debt and equity investments in companies that are operating in the United States every day—producing real value for their customers.

AWP is a bit different. It is a fund that buys up common equity in real estate investments around the world, providing some international diversification. An investment in AWP is an investment in REITs and other real estate companies around the world that generate income through rent and development.

It isn't a game; capital is the grease that keeps the world spinning and provides people with useful goods and services. In exchange for ownership, we get to collect dividends.

Don't fear volatility; use it to your advantage. Lower prices benefit the buyer, not the seller, and we are the buyers.

That's the Income Method.

data.title: OVL: Your Chance To Outperform The S&P While Collecting A 10% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to investing, the S&P (SP500) continues to be the standard index to beat. Probably because it tracks the top 500 U.S. companies. I assume many companies' goal is to one day be included in the S&P.

As an investor, I've never had the goal of beating the index. Instead, I take a simpler approach by selecting quality companies with strong cash flows likely to reward me with income for the foreseeable future.

If you're someone who also focuses on income but wants to potentially beat the index, I may have found the covered call fund to help you do it.

In this article, I discuss what differentiates this covered call fund from its peers, their performance since inception, and why you may want to put this little-known, 10% yielder on your radar.

Who Is OVL?

The overlooked fund I discuss today is Overlay Shares Large Cap Equity ETF (OVL). The ETF is a covered call fund that recently popped up on my radar. While their current yield of 10.49% isn't a standout, their strategy and track record intrigue me.

The ETF is managed by investment manager, Liquid Strategies, a firm founded in 2013. So, still relatively new in my opinion. They currently have 7 ETFs and 2 mutual funds.

OVL is one of them, designed to track the performance of the S&P. OVL was actually designed to outperform the index through capital appreciation and income.

OVL has a decent track record with a little over 6 years since inception. The fund's birth date is September 30, 2019. Their expense ratio sits at 0.79%, and in my opinion, it's solid for a covered call fund.

I look for funds with expense ratios below 1%. This means you'd pay about $80 for every $10,000 invested in OVL.

This is slightly higher than most of NEOS' covered call funds, which have expense ratios around 0.68%. Goldman Sachs' (GS) funds have some of the lowest at around 0.29%.

Strategy

OVL tracks the S&P by actually holding the largest S&P index fund, the Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF (VOO). This is opposite of some peers who use a synthetic strategy to the index by selling calls.

Or those who hold individual equities in their portfolio. OVL also holds U.S. treasuries to generate income, similar to other covered call funds.

But a key difference with OVL is that instead of selling covered call options and using a call spread strategy to generate income, management prefers to sell put options. They also use a put spread strategy to hedge the portfolio, usually over a 2-week period.

Management typically sells short-dated put options on 75% to 100% of the entire portfolio. The reason for the differentiated strategy is put options usually pay more and allow for more upside.

Covered call ETFs that sell call options typically cap their upside; others leave a portion of their portfolio uncovered to potentially see more upside.

Selling puts at lower prices also gives OVL downside protection. However, like most covered call funds, this doesn't make the fund immune to downside, even more so than the index itself would see.

Below is a chart of OVL's performance during the steep sell-off during Liberation Day last April compared to other covered call peers.

OVL saw a higher decline during that time, down 13.16%. The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) saw the lowest at 9.92%. The S&P itself declined 12.14%.

But for the entire year, OVL outperformed its peers, seeing a total return of 18.47%. VOO saw a return of 18.12%. So, OVL did as promised and beat the index, albeit slightly.

JEPI saw the lowest return at 8.17%, while NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) & Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) saw solid performances over 16%.

Below is a chart of OVL's returns from 2020 to 2024. With the exception of 2022, OVL saw strong returns over the four-year period.

And below are the S&P's returns from 2019 to 2024. As promised, OVL has delivered on its goal to outperform the S&P through capital appreciation and income.

While past performance is no indication of future performance, OVL's track record of beating the index for 5 years proves their strategy may be superior to peers. And that it could continue to outperform for the foreseeable future.

NAV Growth & Distributions

OVL has seen healthy NAV growth since inception at 15.38%. Due to market volatility, NAV growth has been negative year-to-date at 3.15%. But paying attention to growth over time is key. And OVL's growth past 1 year prior has been in the double digits.

Prior to 2026, OVL was paying distributions on a quarterly basis. But likely switched to make them more competitive against their peers. In my opinion, this was a good move. I think I can speak for most income-focused investors when I say it's better to get paid on a monthly basis vs. quarterly.

Due to higher inflation, monthly-paying ETFs or equities could be considered more attractive. For 2026, distributions have averaged around $0.46 a share. More importantly, distributions are tax favorable, making them suited for a taxable account.

For the months of January, February, and March, 100% of OVL's distributions were considered return of capital. This means taxes are deferred until your cost basis reaches zero or you decide to sell.

This is important because a lot of covered call funds are a combination of ROC and net investment income. NII subjects investors to an additional tax of 3.8%, depending on your filing status and income threshold.

Below are the thresholds depending on your filing status.

And below is how much you'd pay in taxes should you go over the threshold.

For comparison, SPYI's latest distribution was only a 95% return of capital. GPIX's latest distribution was only 91.4% return of capital and the rest net investment income.

How OVL Will Behave

Because OVL is a covered call fund, the ETF is designed to outperform during flat and rising markets.

During bear markets, OVL will likely underperform, as evidenced by its performance in 2022, when the S&P was down 18.11% compared to 22.06% for the fund.

If we do end up seeing a recession as a result of the ongoing war with Iran, then it's likely OVL's returns may be disappointing for the near term.

But over time, the fund has proven to have a superior strategy by outperforming the S&P since its inception. And I don't see this changing in the foreseeable future.

Risks & Conclusion

A risk for OVL going forward is the potential to continue seeing negative NAV growth. If so, the fund will likely underperform.

And if we do fall into a recession and see a bear market, it's likely for them to see a steeper drawdown than the index itself.

But over time, the S&P will likely rise as it has since its inception. And so should OVL, as it has for the last 5 years.

If you're looking for a covered call fund with market-beating potential that delivers a solid yield, then the Overlay Shares Large Cap Equity ETF may be a solid fund worth adding.

data.title: One Stop Systems: A Pure Play On Edge AI Compute
data.description:
Intro

One Stop Systems (NASDAQ: OSS) is a pure-play manufacturer of ruggedized, datacenter-class AI compute hardware built for military and defense applications. The business is approaching a clear inflection point with gross margins surging to 58.5% alongisde revenue growth of 70.2% YoY this quarter. The company sits at an intersection of two massive trends: a massive hike in defense spending and the military's aggressive pivot to processing AI on the battlefield. With $31.2M in cash, zero debt, and a book-to-bill ratio of 1.2x, OSS presents an attractive entry for exposure to AI edge compute.

Business Overview

OSS designs, manufactures, and sells specialized rugged computer systems that bring datacenter-class AI processing capabilities to environments in which traditional servers would not be able to survive. These systems have applications to armored vehicles, reconnaissance aircrafts, submarines, and autonomous drones. The company recently divested its European distribution subsidiary, Bressner Technology, for $22.4M, giving them a large cash position and repositioning themselves as a pure-play on rugged edge AI compute systems.

Their product portfolio spans three diverse performance tiers. At the high-end, there is the Torrey Break edge supercomputer, which is a PCIe 5.0-based system OSS describes as the "highest performance, most compact supercomputer" capable of surviving harsh environments. This system integrates the latest NVIDIA GPUs and NVMe flash storage in an extremely small form factor. Their mid-tier includes Short-Depth Servers (SDS) that integrate AI compute, data recording, and storage with a cold-plated liquid cooling system. The most ruggedized tier includes the Donati CPU/GPU compute systems and their Cernis intelligent PCIe switches, which are built for combat vehicles that need situational awareness, object recognition, and target identification.

Beyond servers, OSS also produces high-density NVMe flash storage arrays with up to 2 petabytes of hot-swappable containers. They also manufacture PCIe expansion systems and adapters. In November 2025, they launched the industry's first PCIe Gen 6.0 products at SC25. OSS's moat comes from the fact that OSS takes the latest commercially available datacenter components and ruggedizes them for military environments. This is compared to traditional defense electronics competitors, who are ruggedizing older, lower-power embedded processors. OSS has positioned themselves for faster technology refresh cycles and better price-to-performance ratios as they are the only Tier 2 OEM with an NVLink design license.

Contract Acceleration & Macro Catalysts

OSS currently holds a sole-source supplier position for military-grade NVMe flash storage on the Navy's P-8A maritime patrol aircraft under a 5-year, $36M agreement. With lifetime contracted revenue now exceeding $65M, and over $23M awarded since early FY2025 alone, their products are clearly showing signs of accelerated demand. Management has clearly identified a $300M pipeline opportunity for PCIe 5.0 expansion systems on this platform. Other than this, they are also the sole-source supplier of PCIe infrastructure for sonar sensor processing on the Virginia-class submarines. A ~$2M contract renewal in February FY2025 included technology refreshments and prototype deliveries. OSS also holds meaningful optionality with U.S. Army combat vehicles. In January FY2026, they received a $1.2M pre-production order from a "top-tier U.S defense prime" for GPU-accelerated vision and sensor processing systems for Army combat vehicles. Management frames this as "potentially transformative" as the Army's addressable fleet includes 14,000+ vehicles. A separate $1.3M Army 360-degree visualization program using NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin remains under evaluation. Safran Federal Systems has generated ~$1.9M in cumulative orders for rugged short-depth servers supporting naval and aircraft applications, with projected production exceeding $7M over 5 years (up from an initial $3M expectation). USSOCOM signed a Cooperative Research and Development Agreement (CRADA) in May 2025 for advanced edge AI computing in austere maritime environments. An Asian defense contractor has also placed three orders for autonomous maritime USV computing, transitioning from development to production with ~$4M projected through FY2029.

On the commercial side, a medical imaging OEM has moved from a $500k development order to a $2M production order, with management projecting $25M+ over the next 5 years. An autonomous construction/mining customer awarded initial orders in early FY2026 and is targeting $10-$15M over the next 5 years. Additionally, a top-tier aerospace prime has placed a $1.1M initial order for ruggedized Ethernet switches for in-flight entertainment, opening a ~$6.5M five-year pipeline.

Management has cited a total pipeline exceeding $1 billion in target opportunities with a FY2025 book-to-bill ratio of 1.2x, showing their stellar demand from all angles. This contract momentum is entirely due to the fact that OSS' business sits squarely in the path of the largest reorientation of U.S. defense spending we have seen in years. FY2026 marks the first time that the Department of War has created a dedicated budget line for AI and autonomous systems. This budget allows for a total of $13.4B in investments, including $9.4B for unmanned aerial vehicles, $1.7B for maritime autonomous systems, $1.2B for cross-domain software integration, and $734M for underwater capabilities. The broader context is staggering as the FY2026 national defense budget request reached $1.01 trillion, with every service branch increasing AI spending in order to integrate this game-changing technology. Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, stated in his January 2026 AI strategy memo that the military would become an "AI-first warfighting force" and launched seven projects, including "Swarm Forge" (fighting with AI-enabled capabilities) and "Agent Network" (AI-enabled battle management from planning to kill chain execution). The mandate explicitly requires edge computing capabilities as sensor technologies must multiply across weaponry platforms. This computing power must occur at the point of action, not in some distant data centers, due to the intense nature of its use.

OSS' differentiation in this niche comes from three main reasons. First, they have claimed the first-mover status in Time Sensitive Networking (TSN) with multiple product launches while competitors remain in development. Second, its PCIe Gen 6 product launch positions them at the cutting edge of AI interconnect bandwidth. Thirdly, they have a unique ability to ruggedize datacenter-class GPU platforms for edge deployment and fill a gap between companies focused on small-form-factor systems and those focused on enterprise-level HPC. The company has cited more than a $17B global addressable market for rugged edge compute across AI and autonomy applications.

Financial Snapshot

In Q4 FY2025, revenue hit $12.0M, up 70.2% from $7.0M in Q4 FY2024. Their gross profit also exploded to $7.0M from just $0.7M a year prior, as gross margins reached a record 58.5% versus 9.4%. Operating income also swung from a $3.5M loss a year ago to $1.9M in this quarter. GAAP net income from continuing operations was $2.0M ($0.08 per share). For the full year results, revenue grew 31.2% YoY to $32.2M, beating the high-end of prior guidance. Full-year gross margin improved to 49.6% from 2.5%, with net loss from continuing operations dropping to -$3.1M from -$15.2M the year prior.

The margin improvements are the real story here, as it collapsed to negative territory in Q3 FY2024 due to a $6.1M inventory write-down (legacy commercial inventory), then rebuilt quarter by quarter through FY2025. Management guides for ~40% gross margins for FY2026, noting that Q4's exceptional ~58.5% reflected a particularly favorable product mix. The mid-30s to mid-40s range is the more sustainable target. Operating expense discipline remained solid despite their growth investments. They had full-year operating expenses of $19.4M, rising 19% YoY, driven by accelerated R&D spending amidst new program developments. For FY2026, R&D will run at 10-12% of revenue, with total operating expenses expected to moderate.

Management's FY2026 guidance calls for 20-25% revenue growth, ~40% gross margins, and positive full-year EBITDA. Management expressed confidence that "visibility in our pipeline is still strong as we start 2026" and highlighted the benefit of having a large defense budget.

Model

FY2026 Estimates

Metric Q1 FY2026 Q2 FY2026 Q3 FY2026 Q4 FY2026
Revenue $8.5M $10.0M $11.3M $13.2M
Gross Profit $3.3M $4.1M $4.9M $5.9M
Gross Margin 39.0% 41.0% 43.0% 45.0%
Operating Income -$1.7M -$1.0M -$0.2M $0.8M
EBITDA -$1.1M -$0.4M $0.4M $1.4M
Net Income -$1.4M -$0.7M $0.1M $1.1M
EPS -$0.06 -$0.03 $0.00 $0.05

FY2025 vs FY2026 Estimates

Metric FY2025 FY2026 YoY Change
Revenue $32.2M $43.0M

+33.5%
Gross Profit $16.0M $18.2M +13.9%
Gross Margin 49.6% 42.4% -720 bps
Operating Expenses $19.4M $20.3M +4.6%
Operating Income -$3.4M -$2.1M Improving
EBITDA -$1.1M $0.3M Positive
Net Income -$3.1M -$0.9M Improving
EPS -$0.13 -$0.04 Improving

Management guided conservatively in FY2025 and beat the high end of revenue growth. We are applying the same pattern to FY2026, and our 33.5% YoY revenue estimate reflects the P-8A contracts accelerating alongside many other commercial programs entering the production phase. Their book-to-bill of 1.2x as they entered the year confirms their backlog is ready for this growth rate. Their FY2025 gross margin was inflated due to one-time product mix change, and management has guided for ~40% as the FY2026 target, which is in line with our expectations. Management has also guided for positive full-year EBITDA in FY2026. We model a steady run-rate to positive numbers in the back-half of the year, leading to full-year positive EBITDA. Q1 tends to be the lightest quarter, reflecting typical patterns in defense procurement and order timing. We assume ~25M diluted shares and no need for dilutive equity raises, as their current cash position is more than sufficient to fund operations until full profitability is hit.

Valuation

Using our projections for revenue, we can build a forward valuation multiple for OSS. Currently, their enterprise value is ~$157.09M, and using our $43M FY2026 revenue estimate, this places them at a FWD EV/Sales multiple of 3.65x. We can make a peer group of defense supply chain companies that operate in a similar niche using MRCY & CW. Taking the average EV/Sales from these two companies, we arrive at a peer group value of 5.93x. A re-rating of this valuation would put OSS ~62% higher than it currently is. This seems like a reasonable valuation given their macro positioning to benefit from the secular trend of AI automation within the defense industry.

Risks

The biggest risk with OSS is its current customer concentration. Their current revenue momentum is concentrated in just a few key platforms, such as the P-8A Poseidon Reconnaissance Aircraft. It would be very positive to see their products being applied to a broader list of defense platforms. The second largest risk in my eyes is their margins, as OSS is a small-cap that is still in the process of scaling to profitability. It will be important to monitor gross margins and EBITDA margins as we go forward to ensure they continue along their path toward H2 FY2026 EBITDA profitability. Also, due to the fact that they take commercial-off-the-shelf components and ruggedize them, their entire production capacity could be constrained by general semiconductor supply chain problems. If they cannot secure a sufficient amount of GPUs due to data center prioritization, they simply cannot build their systems. I do not foresee this being a problem near-term, but it is a risk that should be discussed.

Conclusion

OSS is a specialized and technically differentiated defense hardware company that has quietly built sole-source positions in some of the most important platforms in the U.S. military's inventory. The Bressner divestiture stripped away the noise in their company and left behind a pure-play edge-AI company with a clean balance sheet, accelerating contract velocity, and a product portfolio that sits at the perfect intersection between the growth in AI automation and the reinvestment into military supply chains. With revenue growing at over 30%, a clear path towards EBITDA positivity, and a forward EV/Sales multiple of ~3.65x, the re-rating opportunity is meaningful. There are many risks, such as customer concentration, margin execution, and project procurement timelines, but they are the normal risks associated with a company in a critical transition. For investors who can stomach the volatility, OSS offers a rare combination of near-term contract acceleration and a perfect product portfolio catered to the modern age of defense.

data.title: My 10 REIT Portfolio That Pays Me $3,000 Each Month
data.description:
Real estate can be a great investment to earn passive income.

All you have to do is collect your monthly rent check, and that's it.

That's at least the perception online.

But the reality is very different, and if you have ever owned rental properties, you can attest to that.

Each new property can require 100, 200, or even more hours of work, even before the property is rented out and generating cash flow.

You have to first educate yourself on your local market. Then you need to meet brokers. Visit lots of different properties. Do your due diligence. Finally, make offers. Negotiate. Close a deal. Secure financing. Do renovations. Market the property. Interview tenants. Do background checks. Have lawyers draft rental agreements. Collect a deposit. Etc.

The work is much more significant than people realize.

And it does not end there.

Once you have your tenant in place, you will still need to deal with maintenance issues, occasional tenant disputes, repairs, insurance claims, mortgage payments, vacancies, potential evictions, etc.

The work never ends. This is not true passive income.

Luckily, there is a better way to invest in rental properties, and that's through REITs.

REITs stand for real estate investment trusts. Lots of them are publicly listed and allow you to invest in rental properties in the same way you would invest in any other sector - by simply buying shares.

Best of all, most REITs are professionally managed and own very large real estate portfolios, often in the billions, resulting in substantial economies of scale. According to some studies, the average management cost of REITs is only 50 basis points of assets per year, or about 2-3x lower than the management cost of private properties:

As such, REITs combine the best of both worlds: stocks and real estate, and allow investors to invest in real estate without its biggest downsides.

They are professionally managed, cost-efficient, and generate significant true passive income.

I just added it up and found out that I am currently earning about $3,000 per month on average from a roughly $690,000 investment in the following 10 REIT investments:

Vesta REIT (VTMX) is only offering a low 2.6% dividend yield, but it still generates me a lot of income because I have invested heavily in it, and my yield on cost is closer to 4%. Even then, the yield is not particularly high, but that is only because the REIT retains about 2/3 of its cash flow to reinvest in growth. Vesta is the leading industrial REIT in Mexico, and it enjoys great opportunities to develop new properties at initial yields in the double digits as it capitalizes on the growing trend of nearshoring. This creates a lot of value for shareholders and is expected to lead to 10% annual FFO and dividend per share growth through 2030. As such, its yield could reach closer to 5% in the not-so-distant future. I expect patience to be richly rewarded.

Vonovia (VNA/VONOY) is the leading apartment landlord in Europe. Its dividend yield is about 5.5% at the current share price, and that’s with a low 55% payout ratio. The company is massive in size, owning 500,000+ apartment units, primarily in Germany, where rents are regulated in a way that results in highly consistent and predictable 3-4% growth each year. The growth continued even during the Great Financial Crisis and the pandemic. As such, its assets are much safer and less cyclical than comparable apartment communities in the US, and this explains why these assets are very popular in the private market and generally trade at ultra-low cap rates in the high 3s or low 4s. But because listed real estate companies are today out of favor, Vonovia is currently offered at a huge 55% discount to NAV and an 11% FFO yield. I think that it is presenting a historic opportunity to buy a stake in German apartment communities at a huge discount.

Crown Castle (CCI) is the highest-yielding cell tower REIT, currently offering a 5.3% dividend yield, which is far more than what its close peers American Tower (AMT) and SBA Communications (SBAC) are offering. Generally, a higher yield implies that the REIT owns weaker and riskier assets, but this is not the case here. CCI is the only cell tower REIT to focus exclusively on the US market, which is the safest market with the highest barriers to entry in the world. I think that the higher yield is largely the result of the REIT’s recent transformation, which has been overlooked by the market. CCI used to trade at a steep discount due to its fiber assets and higher leverage, which weren’t popular, but it has since then gotten rid of them and paid off a bunch of debt. Over time, as the market recognises this, I expect the stock to recover to higher levels.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) just recently slashed its dividend by 40%, and yet, even despite that, it still remains one of my biggest dividend payers. That’s because I unfortunately owned quite a bit of it prior to the cut and have bought more of it since then. Following its crash, the valuation is now the lowest ever for the company, and even with a low 40% payout ratio, its dividend yield is still 6.2%. The life science property sector is severely oversupplied due to the combination of too much speculative development following the pandemic and demand destruction caused by the policy chaos of the current administration. This is a big setback, but I still remain optimistic about the long term. The AI revolution is expected to unleash a golden age in healthcare innovation, making these properties more productive and valuable than ever before to their end users. It is also breaking barriers to entry, and I expect it to lead to an exploration in new biotech formation over the coming years, which will then also increase the competition for these assets. The oversupply is so significant that the recovery will likely take 5+ years, but I think that the wait is worthwhile given how much upside potential it could present in that scenario.

HA Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) is not officially structured as a REIT anymore, but I still included it in this list because it used to be one and still operates a mortgage REIT-like business model, lending capital to renewable energy projects. The company is today offering a 4.7% dividend yield, and importantly, it has guided to grow its FFO per share by 8-10% annually through 2030. This should also lead to rapidly rising dividend income and upside to shareholders.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA / CNNRF) is offering a near 6% dividend yield with a low 55% payout ratio. That’s one of the highest yields of any net lease REITs and one of the lowest payout ratios as well. Its peers like Realty Income (O) offer a lower yield at closer to 5% with a higher payout ratio in the 70s. What makes Canadian Net so much more rewarding? It simply trades at a lower valuation at just 10x FFO. It is the only net lease REIT that exists in Canada, and it has been overlooked by investors. Its dividend has grown by 8% per year on average over the past decade, which is about 2x faster than the growth of its lower-yielding American peers.

Big Yellow Group (BYG/BYLOF) is my largest self-storage investment, and yielding 5.6%, it is resulting in significant dividend income. I have been accumulating it since the Brexit crash of 2016, and all the purchases add up. The story here is simple: the European self-storage market is structurally undersupplied with 20x less storage per capita than in the US, but the concept is now growing in popularity, resulting in lots of high-yielding development opportunities for these REITs. American self-storage REITs went through a similar period of heavy development activity 10-20 years ago, and this period was extremely rewarding for them, resulting in 15-20% annual returns. Big Yellow is now replicating the same model in Europe, and we expect similar growth and upside over the coming decade.

NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) is a smaller position for me, but it still contributes lots of dividend income, given that its yield is 11.5%! It is the highest yielder in my entire portfolio. Typically, such high yields indicate that the REIT is overleveraged and paying an unsustainable dividend, but this is not the case here. The REIT has no debt, and its dividend is well covered with an 83% payout ratio. Rather, the high yield is the result of the REIT’s low valuation. It specializes in cannabis cultivation facilities, which are out of favor today. Many tenants are facing financial difficulties, and lease defaults are frequent in this sector. The good news is that NLCP has done a great job at mitigating risks by focusing on properties in limited-license states with long triple-net leases with above-average operators. The REIT has managed to steadily grow its dividend since going public in 2021, and with the current administration preparing to soon reschedule cannabis as a less dangerous Schedule III drug, the whole sector should soon get a nice boost. We think that the dividend is sustainable.

Sila Realty Trust (SILA) is my biggest dividend investment in the healthcare property sector. The REIT is today offering a 6.9% dividend yield that’s well covered with a safe 70% payout ratio and backed by an underleveraged balance sheet with a 4x debt-to-EBITDA. The REIT owns primarily medical office buildings, surgical facilities, and rehabilitation centers, all under long-term triple net leases with steady annual rent escalations. These properties greatly benefit from the rapidly aging population, resulting in growing recession-resistant demand. The high yield, steady growth, and reasonable valuation make it a great long-term pick for dividend investors in my opinions

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA/BSRTF) is my biggest residential REIT investment, generating me significant passive income. It pays a 4.7% dividend yield, and what’s unique about BSR is that it pays on a monthly basis and often classifies the dividend as return of capital, making it far more tax efficient. I also like that the REIT focuses on affordable apartment communities of the rapidly growing Texas Triangle, resulting in strong long-term rent growth potential. Even then, it is today discounted because these markets have faced some oversupply in recent years, which has hurt fundamentals. I think that this is only temporary and expect an acceleration in rent growth going into 2027 as new supply deliveries will drop back to historically low levels. I expect this to improve the market sentiment and lead to significant upside, given that the REIT is currently trading at a huge 35% discount to its net asset value. I earn significant monthly dividend income while I wait.

Closing Note

These are the exact type of REIT investments that I like to target, as they generate significant and growing passive income.

These are others, of course, as well. When including Foreign REITs, I own close to 50 of them from a universe of 1,000+ opportunities.

As such, our real estate portfolio is very well diversified at High Yield Landlord, and even despite occasionally suffering setbacks, like in the case of Alexandria, we are still able to grow our passive monthly income each year and have so far also beaten our sector benchmarks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vistra Is Capitalizing On America's Energy Boom; Growth Will Improve Valuation Sooner Or Later
data.description:
I have covered Vistra Corp. (VST) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it an interesting buy. Since then, the company reported record 2025 figures and confirmed its 2026 guidance.

Background

Vistra Corp. is a U.S. power player combining a sizable retail electricity business with a diversified generation fleet spread across 18 states and serving close to 5 million customers under several retail brands. The company operates one of the largest generation fleets in the country, with around 44 GW of capacity distributed across different generation facilities, mainly natural gas, but it also has an important presence in coal and nuclear plants. Renewables are residual with 3% of their capacity.

While Vistra's nuclear plants only represent 15% of its capacity, they generated more electricity than its coal plants, with 24% of total production in 2025, highlighting the importance of the Energy Harbor acquisition in 2024. This also implies that the company could greatly benefit from an increase in power demand, as its coal and gas assets are operating between 50% and 60% of their capacity.

In fact, Vistra management expects that 2026 and 2027 will extend this growth, and this trend could be extended even longer as they expect data center demand not to tighten supply-demand until late 2027 or early 2028.

Our primary regions continue to outperform. We maintain our view that annual peak load growth of at least 3% to 5% in ERCOT and low single-digit growth in PJM is achievable through 2030.
